role_summary: Co-founder and captain of the Seattle chapter of the Black Panther Party, 1968-1972.
ai_reading: On the concept axis the corpus calls Nonviolence as Theology vs Armed Self-Defense (raw position values run from -1 at the Nonviolence pole to +1 at the Self-Defense pole), Aaron Dixon's testimony sits at -0.73, far toward the Nonviolence as Theology end and among the strongest pulls in that direction across the entire 136-interview corpus. That is the opposite of where his cultural placement predicts: Black Panther captains are read in historiography as armed-self-defense voices, not theological-nonviolence ones. The four interviewees closer to the Nonviolence as Theology pole than Dixon are his brother Elmer (entry 37, at -1.00), Elbert "Big Man" Howard (BPP co-founder, entry 35, at -0.85), the joint Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson interview (at -0.85), and Kathleen Cleaver (BPP, entry 73, at -0.80); three of those four are Black Panther-identified, exactly the figures whose political reception would predict the opposite pole. Whether the embedding is catching something real about BPP oral testimony reading as more redemptive and community-service-oriented than the party's rhetorical surface (Dixon's Free Breakfast for Children program and his Seattle medical-and-legal clinic are documented in the orientation paragraph below), or a polarity convention in the axis-vector definition that reverses the political mapping, is a question this catalog leaves open for the paper-in-progress that treats these AI-vs-culture mismatches as its central data.
biographical_paragraph: Aaron Dixon was born January 2, 1949 in Chicago and moved with his family to Seattle as a child, settling in the Central District [src: 2]. He attended Garfield High School, the historically Black secondary school at the center of Seattle's Black political life [src: 2]. In April 1967 Stokely Carmichael spoke at Garfield as part of the SNCC speaking tour for Black Power; Dixon dates his political formation to that event [src: 2]. In the weeks following Martin Luther King Jr.'s assassination on April 4, 1968, Dixon and his younger brother Elmer (catalog entry 37) co-founded the Seattle chapter of the Black Panther Party, the first BPP chapter organized outside California [src: 2, 4]. Dixon was nineteen when he took the post of captain [src: 1, 2]. Under his captaincy from 1968 to 1972 the Seattle chapter ran the local instance of the BPP's Free Breakfast for Children program and opened a community health and legal services clinic that continues to operate today as the Carolyn Downs Family Medical Center [src: 2, 4]. In April 1968 Dixon and his brother were in the San Francisco Bay Area for a West Coast Black Student Union conference at which Bobby Seale gave the keynote; during the trip they also attended the funeral of seventeen-year-old Bobby Hutton, the first BPP recruit, killed by Oakland police on April 6 that year [src: 1]. After Seale's speech the brothers asked him to charter a Seattle chapter; it was on that trip that Dixon first met Seale and Kathleen Cleaver (catalog entry 73) [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Aaron Dixon
interview_snippets.lead_in: Dixon dates a turn in his political thinking to a Seattle appearance by the SNCC chairman who had popularized the Black Power slogan:
interview_snippets.quote: And when Stokely Carmichael came to town, I really got fired up.

interview_snippets.speaker: Aaron Dixon
interview_snippets.lead_in: Held in the King County jail on an unlawful-assembly charge, Dixon heard Walter Cronkite announce the assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. on April 4, 1968, and recalls his reaction that night:
interview_snippets.quote: And saying to myself that they killed Martin Luther King, a man of peace. That now is time for something else. And I decided that my picket sign was going to be replaced by a gun.

interview_snippets.speaker: Aaron Dixon
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the Bay Area trip where Bobby Seale gave a Black Student Union keynote, Dixon and his brother approached Seale afterward:
interview_snippets.quote: And my brother and I, after the speech, along with Anthony, where we make a B-line to where he's at. And we tell him we want a Black Panther Party chapter in Seattle.

interview_snippets.speaker: Aaron Dixon
interview_snippets.lead_in: Back in Seattle, Dixon describes standing up the new chapter:
interview_snippets.quote: And so, I began, we began organizing the Seattle chapter. And we opened up our first office and get our phones.

interview_snippets.speaker: Aaron Dixon
interview_snippets.lead_in: Dixon recounts the internal debate over whether community-service work counted as revolutionary, as the Seattle chapter launched its breakfast program in the winter of 1969:
interview_snippets.quote: We get rid of a lot of people and then we start the breakfast for school children program. And there were people who didn't think that was revolutionary serving breakfast for school children.

interview_snippets.speaker: Elmer Dixon
interview_snippets.lead_in: His brother Elmer Dixon (entry 37), co-founder of the Seattle chapter, describes how the leadership roles were assigned at its founding:
interview_snippets.quote: And we organized the chapter. Aaron, my brother was, you know, given the leadership, he was the defense captain.

movement_context: The Seattle chapter Dixon led occupied a structurally distinctive position in the national BPP geography. By April 1968 the party had organized chapters in only a handful of cities (Oakland, Los Angeles, and a small set of early non-California organizing nodes); Bloom and Martin's archival history identifies Seattle as the first formally constituted BPP chapter outside California, founded the same month the assassination of King brought the Panthers a sudden surge of national recruitment interest [src: 4]. The Free Breakfast for Children program the Seattle chapter ran in 1969 onward was the local instance of what had launched in January 1969 at St. Augustine's Episcopal Church in Oakland and within a year was operating in dozens of cities feeding tens of thousands of children weekly [src: 4]. FBI Director J. Edgar Hoover's COINTELPRO program identified the BPP Free Breakfast Program as a specific operational target; internal Bureau memoranda from May 1969, later declassified through the Church Committee's investigation, directed field offices to discredit and disrupt the program by name [src: 5]. The community medical and legal services Dixon's Seattle chapter built (sickle-cell anemia screening, pediatric care, the legal-aid clinic that grew into the Carolyn Downs center) were the kind of community-service infrastructure Bloom and Martin argued constituted the truer historical face of the BPP than the armed-confrontation imagery that dominated contemporaneous press coverage [src: 4]. That historiographical reframing sits beneath the AI's-reading observation above: Dixon's testimony reads as theological-nonviolence-oriented because the work he led WAS that, not because the embedding misread him.
legacy_and_reception: The Carolyn Downs Family Medical Center, named for the Seattle BPP activist and clinic founder who died of cancer in 1974, remains in continuous operation in Seattle's Central District as a federally qualified community health center; it is operated by Sea Mar Community Health Centers and is among the few directly continuous institutional legacies of the BPP's community-service infrastructure still functioning under its original program model [src: 6]. After the Seattle chapter dissolved in the mid-1970s Dixon worked as a community organizer in Seattle, founding the youth services program Central House [src: 2]. He ran for the United States Senate from Washington State as the Green Party candidate in 2006 against incumbent Maria Cantwell, drawing roughly twenty-two thousand votes in the general election [src: 7]. His 2012 memoir My People Are Rising, published by Haymarket Books, became the primary first-person archival document of the Seattle chapter's history and is paired in the scholarly literature on the BPP with the 2005 University of Washington Civil Rights and Labor History Project oral history Dixon recorded with project director Trevor Griffey [src: 8]. The Library of Congress oral history at catalog entry 1, recorded in Seattle on May 11, 2013 by David Cline for the Civil Rights History Project, is the third major recorded interview in his archival record [src: 1].

gallery.caption: Bobby Hutton, the first recruit of the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense, killed by Oakland police April 6, 1968 at age seventeen. Dixon's April 1968 trip to Oakland for Hutton's funeral was where the Seattle organizers met the BPP central committee and arranged the formal chartering of the Seattle chapter.
gallery.license: Public domain (U.S. publication 1960s, no copyright notice)

gallery.alt: Black Panther Party Free Breakfast for Children program flyer, Washington D.C., 1970
gallery.caption: Black Panther Party Free Breakfast for Children program flyer, Washington D.C. chapter, announcing the opening of a second program location at 2804 14th Street NW. The Seattle chapter ran its own Free Breakfast program through the Madrona neighborhood and the Central District during the same period.
gallery.license: Public domain (U.S. publication 1931-1977 without copyright notice)

gallery.alt: Aaron Dixon (left) with his brother Elmer Dixon at the Northwest African American Museum in Seattle, 2012
gallery.caption: Aaron Dixon (left) and Elmer Dixon (catalog entry 37) at the Northwest African American Museum in Seattle, September 2012, during the publication tour for Aaron's memoir My People Are Rising. The brothers co-founded the Seattle chapter of the Black Panther Party in April 1968.
